figures.gloss: The incarnate Son of God, fully divine and fully human, who preached the Kingdom of God, was crucified under Pontius Pilate, and resurrected on the third day. He is the Logos through whom the universe was made, voluntarily entering history.
figures.facet: God does not merely send an avatar or a message; God becomes meat, suffers a humiliating, state-sanctioned execution, and validates the material world by resurrecting in a physical body, destroying the barrier between transcendent deity and human agony.
figures.provenance: The Canonical Gospels; Epistles of Paul; Nicene Creed.

figures.gloss: The foundational Christian mystery: one God existing eternally as three distinct, co-equal persons. They share a single divine essence but interact in dynamic, self-giving love, fundamentally defining the Christian understanding of the divine nature.
figures.facet: It shatters strict unitarian monotheism without collapsing into polytheism. God's inner life is intrinsically relational; love is not just something God does, but the ontological reality of what God is before anything was created.
figures.provenance: Gospel of Matthew; First Council of Nicaea; Athanasian Creed; Augustine's De Trinitate.

figures.gloss: The instrument of Roman torture transformed into the ultimate symbol of victory and reconciliation. Upon it, Christ bore the weight of human sin and death, offering himself as a spotless lamb to reconcile creation to the Creator.
figures.facet: The axis mundi is not a majestic world tree or cosmic mountain, but a bloody instrument of execution. Salvation is achieved through ultimate defeat, vulnerability, and the shedding of innocent blood, turning worldly power entirely upside down.
figures.provenance: Pauline Epistles; Gospel accounts of the Passion; Anselm's Cur Deus Homo.

figures.gloss: The young Jewish woman chosen to conceive the Son of God through the Holy Spirit. Venerated as the new Eve, her fiat ('let it be done') reversed the disobedience of Eden, making her the preeminent saint and intercessor.
figures.facet: Unlike primordial earth mothers who give birth to the cosmos through blind nature, her motherhood is a conscious, dangerous act of human consent to the divine will. The infinite God requires the permission of a teenage girl to enter history.
figures.provenance: Gospels of Luke and Matthew; Council of Ephesus (431 AD); Protoevangelium of James.

figures.gloss: The highest of angels who fell through pride, becoming the adversary of God and the deceiver of humanity. He tempts Christ in the desert, prowls the earth seeking souls to ruin, and is definitively defeated by the resurrection.
figures.facet: He is not a co-equal evil god in a dualistic war. He is a created, deeply limited being—a rebellious subordinate whose very attempts to destroy God's plan are ironically used by God to bring about ultimate redemption.
figures.provenance: Book of Revelation; Book of Job; Gospels; Augustine's City of God.

figures.gloss: The central sacrament of the Christian faith, instituted by Christ at the Last Supper. Bread and wine are consecrated to become the body and blood of Christ, offered as spiritual nourishment to the faithful.
figures.facet: It radically transforms ancient sacrificial meals. The deity is not just the recipient of the sacrifice but the sacrifice itself, voluntarily broken and consumed by the worshippers to incorporate them directly into the divine life.
figures.provenance: 1 Corinthians 11; The Didache; Justin Martyr's First Apology.

figures.gloss: The ancestral catastrophe in Eden where humanity rebelled against God's command, fracturing the original harmony of creation. This act infected all subsequent generations with a fallen nature, introducing death, labor, and spiritual estrangement from the Creator.
figures.facet: Death and suffering are not natural, intended features of the cosmos, nor the fault of a flawed demiurge. They are the direct, tragic consequence of human moral agency and the abuse of free will.

figures.gloss: The eschatological hope that at the end of time, Christ will return to judge the living and the dead. Believers will be raised not as disembodied spirits, but in glorified, physical bodies to live in a renewed, physical heaven and earth.
figures.facet: It utterly rejects the Platonic and Gnostic idea that salvation is the soul escaping the prison of the body. The Christian endgame is the eternal redemption and glorification of the physical, material world.
figures.provenance: 1 Corinthians 15; Book of Revelation; Apostles' Creed.

edges.note: Christ is the second person of the Triune Godhead, co-eternal with the Father and the Spirit.
The cross is the defining altar upon which the incarnate God accomplishes the cosmic atonement.
Christ's perfect obedience and humility perfectly invert Satan's absolute pride and rebellion.
Mary is understood as the 'New Eve' whose obedient consent unties the knot of Eve's original disobedience.
The Eucharist is the perpetual, bloodless re-presentation of the bloody sacrifice accomplished once on the cross.
GOLD SEAM: Literal same-god identity; the specific Christian elaboration of the shared Abrahamic Messiah.
GOLD SEAM: Early Christian theology radically identified Jesus directly with Yahweh, applying Old Testament texts about the God of Israel to the crucified carpenter.
GOLD SEAM: Both are dying-and-rising figures whose resurrection conquers death and guarantees the eternal life of their followers.
GOLD SEAM: The iconography of Isis nursing Horus in Ptolemaic Egypt profoundly influenced early Christian visual depictions of the Madonna and Child.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary who seeks to ruin the supreme deity's good creation through deception, though Christian theology denies him co-eternal status.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic tree upon which a divine figure hangs and suffers to gain ultimate victory and cosmic reconciliation.
GOLD SEAM: Both feature the ritual ingestion of wine as the blood of a suffering god, though the Eucharist transfigures ecstatic frenzy into a sober sacrament of communion.
GOLD SEAM: The eschatological vision of a final bodily resurrection and a remade, purified world entered Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity through Persian Zoroastrian contact.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme tempter who confronts the savior in the wilderness, offering worldly dominion to distract them from their world-saving mission.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions trace the flawed, difficult nature of the present world to a premature, rebellious act that fractured the creator's original design.
GOLD SEAM: The beloved, innocent son of the divine whose tragic, unjust death shifts the cosmic age and causes the whole world to weep.
GOLD SEAM: Spanish colonizers intentionally conflated the Mesoamerican lord of the dead with the Christian Devil, projecting moral evil onto a morally neutral cosmic function.
GOLD SEAM: In the Americas, indigenous populations and missionaries frequently synthesized the Christian cross directly with the Mesoamerican axis mundi.
GOLD SEAM: As Christianity displaced paganism, virgin goddesses like Athena were frequently overlaid with Marian devotion, most famously in the conversion of the Parthenon into a church of Mary.
GOLD SEAM: A structural resonance where ultimate reality manifests in three distinct but inseparable dimensions (Father/Dharmakaya, Son/Nirmanakaya, Spirit/Sambhogakaya).
GOLD SEAM: The Mass is the Roman elaboration of the shared Christian eucharist — same rite, same institution narrative, with transubstantiation as the distinctively Catholic metaphysical claim.
GOLD SEAM: The apparitions are Catholicism's living extension of the same Theotokos — the God-bearer of the Councils appearing as a localized, speaking presence.
GOLD SEAM: The Divine Liturgy is the Byzantine elaboration of the shared Christian eucharist — the same sacrament staged as realized eschatology.
GOLD SEAM: The same incarnate Christ of the shared tradition — the non-Chalcedonian family diverges at 451 over how, not whether, God and man are one in him.

GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — the Reformation is a reform within Latin Christianity, and Luther's theology of the cross re-centers the whole tradition on the Pauline atonement, claiming recovery, not new revelation.
GOLD SEAM: Same Christ, same creeds — Protestantism keeps Nicaea and Chalcedon intact and disputes only the mediating apparatus between Christ and the believer.

GOLD SEAM: Restorationist descent — the movement arose inside Second Great Awakening Protestantism and claims to restore, not reform, Christ's primitive church; the same Christ, radically reframed by an embodied Father and a non-Nicene Godhead.
GOLD SEAM: Two readings of the same Levitical premise that life resides in blood — the Eucharist centers on drinking the sacred blood, the Witness taboo forbids taking blood in at all.
Shaker doctrine reads Ann Lee as the second, female appearing of the Christ-spirit that first dwelt in Jesus — the two appearings completing the Father-Mother image of God.

GOLD SEAM: Divine Principle holds that Jesus won spiritual salvation but was crucified before marrying; the True Parents claim to complete the physical restoration he left undone — a doctrinal graft directly onto Christology.
The text's hinge is a rereading of Genesis 3 — the Fall as Lucifer's sexual seduction of Eve — making misused love the root sin and the restored God-centered family its cure.

Indemnity generalizes atonement: where the Cross pays once for all, tanggam distributes restitution across every believer's acts — restoration by accumulated conditions rather than a single sacrifice.
GOLD SEAM: the church's own name styles Kimbangu Christ's 'special envoy,' and his arrest, colonial trial, and thirty-year passion are read liturgically through the trial of Christ.
GOLD SEAM: Kimbanguist doctrine maps the founder and his three sons onto the Trinity — an attested, internally central, ecumenically contested re-inhabiting of Nicene structure.
GOLD SEAM: the same Jesus, with the deity subtracted — the INC honors Christ as savior and mediator while denying precisely the incarnation claim that defines Nicene Christianity.
GOLD SEAM: the INC defines itself against the Trinity as a post-apostolic corruption — the seam to Christianity runs through the doctrine it exists to refuse.
GOLD SEAM: the Rizalista churches explicitly identify Rizal with Christ — a Tagalog Christ whose December 30 martyrdom re-enacts the Passion on Philippine soil.

GOLD SEAM: Fang Bwiti's syncretic layer is attested history — mission Christianity (1900s-40s) folded into the liturgy, God and Christ absorbed under Fang names without displacing eboga; the missions fought it, the chapels kept both.

Both are eaten sacraments through which the divine becomes present in the communicant — but Bwiti stakes everything on direct vision where the eucharist works by faith; the banzie themselves draw the contrast.
In Christianized chapels the Sister of God is read alongside the Virgin — a feminine mediator before a remote godhead; the equation is reported unevenly across branches, so the seam stays faint.

GOLD SEAM: Rātana liturgy invokes Te Matua, Te Tama, Te Wairua Tapu — and with them ngā Anahera Pono, the Faithful Angels, and the Māngai himself; the mainstream churches broke with the movement in the mid-1920s over precisely this extension of Trinitarian prayer.

GOLD SEAM: a church built wholly of Christian scripture in Māori, received from the missions and re-covenanted — yet with its centre of gravity in Ihowa and the prophets of Israel's exile rather than in the missionaries' own churches, which Te Kooti's people refused.
Pasqually's Traite is an esoteric exegesis of Genesis: reintegration is the doctrine of the Fall run in reverse, restoring Adam's estate rather than atoning for his sin.
Documented transposition: Comte deliberately rebuilt Marian devotion — Madonna iconography, the Virgin-Mother utopia, a female moral providence — around Clotilde de Vaux and personified Humanity.
Comte modeled sociolatry's nine social sacraments explicitly on the Catholic sacramental system — the borrowing Huxley skewered as 'Catholicism minus Christianity' (1869).

dossier.essence: The global religion confessing Jesus of Nazareth as the Christ — the incarnate Son of God, crucified under Pontius Pilate around 30 CE and raised bodily from the dead. Beginning as a Jewish messianic movement, it became the largest religion on earth, roughly 2.3–2.4 billion people across Catholic, Orthodox, Protestant, and independent churches. Its central claim is that in this one execution and resurrection God reconciled the world to himself.
dossier.cosmos: One God, eternally triune (Father, Son, Holy Spirit), creates the world from nothing and good; humanity's primal rebellion (the Fall) introduces sin and death; God enters history as a particular human being, and the cross and resurrection accomplish redemption. History is linear and ends in Christ's return, the resurrection of the body, final judgment, and a renewed creation — not the soul's escape from matter but matter's glorification. Traditions differ on the interim state of the dead (immediate presence with God, purgation, or sleep until the resurrection), but heaven and hell mark the final alternatives.
dossier.practice: Entry by baptism; sustained by the Eucharist and weekly assembly on Sunday, the day of resurrection; ordered by a liturgical year climaxing at Easter. The ethical core is the double command — love of God and neighbor — elaborated through traditions of almsgiving, monasticism, and mission. Institutional forms span the episcopal hierarchies of Rome and the Orthodox East to congregational independency; sacramental counts and church order divide the families, while the Nicene Creed largely unites them.
dossier.canon: The Bible: the Old Testament (the Hebrew Scriptures, with deuterocanonical books in Catholic and Orthodox use) and the twenty-seven-book New Testament; the ecumenical creeds function as the canonical rule of interpretation.
dossier.reflects: Transcendence reconciled with history through one mediating person — the incarnational impulse, with victory located paradoxically in defeat.
sources: Jaroslav Pelikan, 'The Christian Tradition: A History of the Development of Doctrine', 5 vols. (1971–1989)
Diarmaid MacCulloch, 'Christianity: The First Three Thousand Years' (2009)
